paper_abstract: Abstract The chemical stability, electronic structures, mechanical properties and Debye temperature of Fe–Mn– Al alloys were investigated using first-principles calculations. The formation enthalpy and cohesive energy are negative for Fe–Mn–Al alloys, showing that they are thermodynamically stable. FeAl has the lowest formation enthalpy, indicating that FeAl is the most stable alloy in the Fe–Mn–Al system. The partial density of states, total density of states and electron density distribution maps were used to analyze the physical properties of the Fe–Mn– Al alloys. A combination of mainly covalent and metallic bonds exists in these Fe–Mn–Al alloys, resulting in good electronic conductivity, high melting points, and high hardness. These alloys display disparate anisotropy due to the calculated different shapes of the 3D curved surface of the Young’s modulus and anisotropic index. FeAl has the highest bulk modulus, shear modulus and Yong’s modulus of 187.1, 119.8 and 296.2 GPa, respectively. Further, the Debye temperatures and sound velocity of these Fe–Mn–Al compounds were explored.
paper_title: Properties of Fe–Mn–Al alloys with different Mn contents using density functional theory

citation_sentences: As shown in Table 1, the cohesive energy of these $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } _ { x } \mathbf { A } \mathbf { l }$ alloys varies from - 6.568 to $- \ 6 . 9 4 7 \ \mathrm { e V \cdot a t o m } ^ { - 1 }$ , and the value of the formation enthalpy of these alloys varies from - 0.215 to $- \ 0 . 3 9 1 \ \mathrm { e V \cdot a t o m } ^ { - 1 }$ . By comparing the cohesive energy values for $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } _ { x } \mathbf { A } \mathbf { l }$ system, it is found that FeAl is most stable from the viewpoint of cohesive energy. However, the thermodynamic stability is dependent on the formation enthalpy of the compound. The lower the negative formation enthalpy values are, the more stable the crystal structure will be. Figure 2 depicts the calculated cohesive energy and formation enthalpy for different Mn contents. It can be seen that both the cohesive energy and formation enthalpy increase with Mn content. Further, the formation enthalpy value for FeAl is the lowest. Therefore, in the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathrm { M n } _ { x } \mathrm { A l }$ system, FeAl is the most stable.

paper_title: Properties of Fe–Mn–Al alloys with different Mn contents using density functional theory
citation_sentences: The calculated PDOS and TDOS of the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } _ { x } \mathbf { A } 1$ system are shown in Fig. 3. All these Fe–Mn–Al alloys have metallic features because they exhibit nonzero density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level. The magnetic characteristics were determined from the spin-polarized TDOS. Generally, the down and up states are almost symmetric, but a comparison of the down and up states shows that these alloys have magnetic characteristics due to the dissymmetric down and up states. Fe and Mn are transition metals, which only need to consider the role of the outermost electrons. From the DOS for Fe, it is affected by the

Fe-d bands primarily. In Fig. 3, the up and down DOS curves of these Fe–Mn–Al alloys near the Fermi level are divided into one sharp peak mainly. It is found that the sharp peak is located on the Fermi level. There are many low peaks near the sharp peak. These peaks are attributed primarily to the Fe-d and Mn-d states. For FeAl, the band range from - 5 to 1 eV is contributed to the Al-p bands and Fe-d bands mainly. For MnAl, the band range from - 5 to 1 eV is determined by the Al-p bands and Mn-d bands mainly. For other Fe–Mn–Al alloys, the band range from - 5 to 1 eV is determined by the Al-p bands, Fe-d bands and Mn-d bands mainly. Near the Fermi level, the TDOS of FeAl and MnAl are composed mainly of the low Al-p states hybridized with the Fe-d and Mn-d states, respectively. The TDOS of the other Fe–Mn–Al alloys indicate that there is hybridization mainly between the low Al-p, Fe-d, and Mn-d states. Additionally, the FeAl and MnAl PDOS curves indicate that the Fermi surface is primarily due to the contribution of the Fe-d states and Mnd states, but also involve low Al-p states. The curves of PDOS of the other Fe–Mn–Al alloys show that the Fermi surface is due to the contribution of the Fe-d states and Mnd states, but also involve low Al-p states.

citation_sentences: The calculated total electron density distribution maps and electron density difference distribution maps for the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } _ { x } \mathbf { A } 1$ system are shown in Fig. 4. From Fig. 4a, c, e, g, i, Fe, Mn, and Al of core regions have smaller interstitial area and larger values. And there are covalent bonds between Fe, Mn and Al atoms. From these total electron density distribution maps, the elongated contours along the Al–Fe, Al–Mn and Fe–Mn bond axes indicate that covalent interactions exist. More details about chemical bonding characteristics can be found from the electron density difference distribution in Fig. 4b, d, f, h, j. Some electrons are delocalized and distributed throughout the circular map for the area between Al, Mn, and Fe atoms, indicating the features of covalent and metallic bonding. The electrons are actually located around Mn, Fe, and Al atoms as indicated in blue color; this shows the existence of covalent bonds between the atoms. According to above discussions, these Fe–Mn–Al alloys have magnetic characteristics, and the alloys have combinations of covalent and metallic bonds. It can be concluded that these alloys have weaker covalent bonds effect. These bonding features could lead to high melting points and high hardness.

citation_sentences: where k denotes the Pugh’s modulus ratio $\left( { k = G / B } \right)$ . The calculated results for the $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathrm { M n } _ { x } \mathrm { A l }$ system are listed in Table 2. FeAl and MnAl have the highest and lowest hardness of 16.5 and 5.0 GPa, respectively. In Table 2, with the increase in Mn content, from $\mathrm { F e } _ { 0 . 8 7 5 } \mathrm { M n } _ { 0 . 1 2 5 } \mathrm { A l }$ to $\mathrm { F _ { e 0 . 1 2 5 } M n _ { 0 . 8 7 5 } A l }$ , it can be found that $\mathrm { F e } _ { 0 . 5 } \mathrm { M n } _ { 0 . 5 } \mathrm { A l }$ has the lowest $C _ { 1 1 }$ value of 225.0 GPa which shows that $\mathrm { F e } _ { 0 . 5 } \mathrm { M } -$ $\mathtt { n } _ { 0 . 5 } \mathrm { A l }$ is highly compressible under uniaxial stress along the crystallographic x-axis $\left( \varepsilon _ { 1 1 } \right)$ . It seems that $C _ { 1 2 }$ has a maximum value, $C _ { 4 4 }$ has a minimum value, E has a minimum value and Poisson ration reaches a maximum value for $\mathrm { F e _ { 0 . 5 } M n _ { 0 . 5 } A l }$ alloy, indicating that $\mathrm { F e } _ { 0 . 5 } \mathrm { M n } _ { 0 . 5 } \mathrm { A l }$ has the highest hardness and the best ductility in these compounds. The elastic constants for different Mn contents are listed in Figs. 5, 6 and 7. From Fig. 5, it can be found that the Young’s modulus and $C _ { 1 1 } - C _ { 1 2 }$ follow a similar variation trend. As shown in Fig. 6, the shear modulus and $C _ { 4 4 }$ show quite a similar variation trend, but the bulk modulus, shear modulus and $C _ { 4 4 }$ have no clear relationship. Further, from Fig. 7, the Poisson’s ratio and B/G show a similar variation trend.

From Table 3, MnAl has the strongest anisotropy of shear modulus in (100), (010), and (001) planes, and the $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 }$ and $A _ { 3 }$ values are 4.97. In (100), (010), and (001) planes, FeAl has the weakest anisotropy of the shear modulus among these Fe–Mn–Al alloys, and $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 }$ and $A _ { 3 }$ values are 1.96. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8, the changes in $A _ { \mathrm { G } }$ and $A ^ { \mathrm { U } }$ follow the same trend.

The partial density of states, total density of states and electron density distribution maps were used to analyze the physical properties of the Fe–Mn– Al alloys. A combination of mainly covalent and metallic bonds exists in these Fe–Mn–Al alloys, resulting in good electronic conductivity, high melting points, and high hardness. These alloys display disparate anisotropy due to the calculated different shapes of the 3D curved surface of the Young’s modulus and anisotropic index. FeAl has the highest bulk modulus, shear modulus and Yong’s modulus of 187.1, 119.8 and 296.2 GPa, respectively. Further, the Debye temperatures and sound velocity of these Fe–Mn–Al compounds were explored.
paper_title: Properties of Fe–Mn–Al alloys with different Mn contents using density functional theory
citation_sentences: More details about the anisotropic properties of Young’s modulus on (001) and (110) planes are illustrated in Fig. 9. On these planes, the Young’s modulus has strong directional dependence. At the [100], [010], [001] and [110] directions, $\mathrm { F e } _ { 0 . 1 2 5 } \mathrm { M n } _ { 0 . 8 7 5 } \mathrm { A l }$ shows the maximum Young’s modulus, and MnAl shows the minimum Young’s modulus. For these alloys, the planar contours on the (001) and (110) planes are not similar to a spherical shape, which indicates that the Young’s modulus of these alloys on (001) and (110) planes shows strong anisotropy.

caption: Fig. 10 Variations in hardness and Debye temperature of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathrm { M n } -$ $_ x \mathrm { A l }$ system
paper_abstract: Abstract The chemical stability, electronic structures, mechanical properties and Debye temperature of Fe–Mn– Al alloys were investigated using first-principles calculations. The formation enthalpy and cohesive energy are negative for Fe–Mn–Al alloys, showing that they are thermodynamically stable. FeAl has the lowest formation enthalpy, indicating that FeAl is the most stable alloy in the Fe–Mn–Al system. The partial density of states, total density of states and electron density distribution maps were used to analyze the physical properties of the Fe–Mn– Al alloys. A combination of mainly covalent and metallic bonds exists in these Fe–Mn–Al alloys, resulting in good electronic conductivity, high melting points, and high hardness. These alloys display disparate anisotropy due to the calculated different shapes of the 3D curved surface of the Young’s modulus and anisotropic index. FeAl has the highest bulk modulus, shear modulus and Yong’s modulus of 187.1, 119.8 and 296.2 GPa, respectively. Further, the Debye temperatures and sound velocity of these Fe–Mn–Al compounds were explored.
paper_title: Properties of Fe–Mn–Al alloys with different Mn contents using density functional theory
citation_sentences: The sound velocities and Debye temperature of $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } _ { x } \mathbf { A } \mathbf { l }$ system were calculated and the values are listed in Table 4. In general, the Debye temperature can help in understanding the thermal properties and chemical bonding features of compounds. For a crystal structure, the Debye temperature can reflect the strength of covalent bonding. From Table 4, it can be found that FeAl has the largest $\boldsymbol { \Theta } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ of 652.5 K. Hence, it can be concluded that FeAl has stronger covalent bonds than other $\mathrm { F e } _ { 1 - x } \mathbf { M } \mathbf { n } _ { x } \mathbf { A } \mathbf { l }$ alloys. MnAl has the highest B/G ratio (2.52) and the lowest $\boldsymbol { \Theta } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ (504.1 K), which shows the strongest metallic character. With the increase in Mn content, from $\mathrm { F e } _ { 0 . 8 7 5 } \mathrm { M n } _ { 0 . 1 2 5 } \mathrm { A l }$ to $\mathrm { F e _ { 0 . 1 2 5 } M n _ { 0 . 8 7 5 } A l , F e _ { 0 . 5 } M n _ { 0 . 5 } A l }$ has the highest B/G ratio (2.28) and the lowest $\boldsymbol { \Theta } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ (533.3 K), which shows the strongest metallic character and the weakest covalent bonds in these compounds.
